segments.s212-g9: هوا is the subject 'the air/sky'. تیره‌گون is a predicative adjective 'dark-hued/gloomy' describing هوا. بود is the past copula 'was'. از تیر ماه is a prepositional/time phrase: از + ماه (the month Tir) indicates the temporal/circumstantial cause ('in/through the month Tir' or 'because of the month Tir'). Word order is Subject + predicate-adjective + copula + prepositional phrase.
segments.s212-g10: همی is an archaic/adverbial marker giving continuous or iterative aspect ('kept/was continuously'). گشت is past of گشتن here meaning 'turned/rolled/was moving' (intransitive). بر کوه is a locative prepositional phrase 'over/on the mountain' (بر = upon). ابر سیاه is a noun + adjective 'black cloud(s)' (Persian places adjective after noun). Overall: 'black clouds kept moving over the mountain.'
segments.s212-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بازور is an adverb 'by force/with difficulty' modifying شد. در کوه شد is a verbal phrase literally 'became in the mountain' = 'entered/was inside the mountain' (شد used as past of شدن/become/come to be). در زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time / at the moment', placed after the verb for emphasis. The clause thus means 'when, by force, (he) got into the mountain, at that moment...'.
segments.s212-g12: برآمد is a past intransitive verb 'arose/issued forth'. یکی is an indefinite numeral 'a certain / one' introducing the following items. برف و باد are coordinated nouns 'snow and wind'. دمان modifies باد (باد دمان) as a noun or descriptive word indicating a strong gust/stormy wind; together the phrase reads 'there arose a snow and a (gale) wind.' Word order: verb + indefinite determiner + coordinated noun phrase + modifier.
segments.s212-g13: همه is a universal determiner 'all/every'. دست is the noun 'hands' (here idiomatically 'strength/working hands' but literal 'hands' works). آن نیزه‌داران is the possessed noun phrase 'those spear‑bearers' (آن = that/those + نیزه‌داران = spear‑bearers); Persian allows the possessor to follow the possessed noun. ز کار is a causal/prepositional phrase 'from/because of the work' (ز is poetic form of از). The sequence reads 'all the hands of those spear‑bearers, from work...'.
segments.s212-g14: فروماند is a past/resultative 'were worn out/failed/grew exhausted' (subject implied from previous phrase). از برف gives the cause 'because of/from the snow' and در کارزار is the locative 'in the battlefield'. Together: '...were exhausted because of the snow in the battlefield.'
segments.s212-g15: ازان is the compound prepositional demonstrative 'from that' (referring back to previous events). رستخیز is a noun 'uprising/tumult' (here 'tumult' of battle). و connects to دم زمهریر: دم is 'breath/blast' and زمهریر is a poetic term 'bitter cold / piercing cold'; together 'the blast of bitter cold'. This whole phrase is an ablative causal/temporal phrase 'from that tumult and the blast of bitter cold'.
segments.s212-g16: خروش یلان is a genitive phrase 'the roar of the heroes' (خروش = roar/uproar; یلان = heroes). بود is the past copula 'was' linking this phrase to the following coordinated noun. و باران تیر adds 'and a rain of arrows' — باران used figuratively as 'a shower' and تیر = 'arrows'. Overall: 'there was the heroes' roar and a shower of arrows.'
segments.s212-g169: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning "when." بشنید is a past tense 3rd-person singular verb "he heard." گودرز is the subject (proper name). برزد is an archaic/perfective verb here meaning "struck forth/uttered" and takes خروش as its direct object: "he uttered a cry." The word order is typical Persian (temporal marker + verb + subject + verb + object) with some poetic inversion.
segments.s212-g170: زمین آمد is a verbal clause where آمد (came/arrived) describes the earth's reaction—"the earth came" meaning it quivered/rose. از بانگ اسپان بجوش is a causal/adverbial phrase: از (+ noun) "from/on account of" the بانگ (cry/sound) of the اسپان (horses), بجوش (a stative/preterite participial sense here = "seethed/rose in commotion"). The verb آمد links to the following imagery of agitation caused by the horses' cry.
segments.s212-g171: همه (all/everyone) is a universal quantifier modifying مهتران (the elders/chiefs). جامه کردند چاک is a light-verb construction: جامه (garment) + کردند (they made/did) + چاک (a tear) — together "they tore their garments." The verb کردند is past tense plural referring back to مهتران.
segments.s212-g172: بسربر is an adverbial/compound meaning "head‑first / on the head" (headlong). پراگند is an adjective/participle "scattered" describing خاک (earth/dust). The sequence places the modifier بسربر before پراگند خاک with the proper name گودرز inserted: "Goudarz fell headlong, his dust scattered." The phrase is telegraphic/poetic with omitted linking verbs.
segments.s212-g173: همی گفت is a frequentative or continuous narration marker + verb: همی (still/continually) + گفت (he said) — "he kept saying." کاندر جهان (کان + در جهان) is a relativizing/locative phrase "in which / in the world." کس ندید is a negative existential: کس (no one/anyone) + ندید (did not see) — "no one has seen." Word order follows Persian SOV but with poetic contractions.
segments.s212-g174: به پیران سر این بد is a nominal phrase: به پیران (among/for the elders) + سر (case/head, used metaphorically = "instance/lot") + این بد (this bad one). که بر من رسید is a relative clause "which came upon me": که (that) + بر (upon) + من (me) + رسید (reached/befell). Together: "no one among the elders has seen such a bad case as this that befell me."
segments.s212-g175: چرا بایدم is a rhetorical interrogative: چرا (why) + بایدم (I must / should I) — literally "why should I." زنده (alive) is the predicate/adverbial state. با پیر سر links the speaker with the fallen elder: با (with) + پیر (the old man) + سر (head) — "with the old man's head" (i.e., "while the old man lies with his head ..."). The clause sets up the contrast that follows.
segments.s212-g176: بخاک اندر افگنده is a passive/perfective descriptive phrase: بخاک (to the ground) + اندر (into/in) + افگنده (cast/laid) — "cast headlong to the earth." چندین پسر at the end names the sufferers: چندین (several/many a) + پسر (sons). In poetry the agent/affected may be compacted: "many sons thrown (to the ground)."
segments.s212-g105: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source/cause. بانگ is a noun 'cry/crying sound' and takes the following genitive phrase (implicitly بانگِ سواران...). سواران (horsemen) is the head noun and پرخاشخر is a postpositive adjective modifying سواران ('the quarrelsome/ aggressive riders'); the whole phrase functions as a noun phrase governed by ز.
segments.s212-g106: درخشیدن here is a verbal noun/infinitive (masdar) used nominally: 'the shining/flash.' تیغ و زخم تبر is a coordinated noun phrase: تیغ ('sword') is the first coordinated member, و is the coordinating conjunction, and زخم تبر is a noun + noun (زخم 'wound' with تبر 'axe' in an attributive/genitive relation) — 'wounds of the axe.' The clause is verbless and nominative in tone, common in poetry: 'From X, the shining of swords and wounds of axes.'
segments.s212-g107: This is a list of sources/agents introduced by prepositions: the initial ز 'from' (poetic) begins the series; پیکان 'arrow' is the first item; the coordinating و links items. The second item repeats the preposition از 'from' before گرز (mace/club), then more coordinated items ژوپین (a padded jacket/gambeson, here listed as a weapon/armour item) and تیر (arrow/bolt). The alternating ز / از + noun pattern is a poetic enumerative device meaning 'from X and from Y and ...'.
segments.s212-g108: زمین (the earth/ground) is the grammatical subject, followed by شد 'became' (past of شدن). بکردار here functions as a predicative adjective/phrase meaning 'in the guise/form/character of' (an archaic/poetic usage), and دریای قیر is a genitive phrase 'sea of pitch/tar.' The clause thus reads 'the ground became (as) a sea of tar.'
segments.s212-g109: همه ('all/the whole') fronted for emphasis modifies دشت 'plain.' بی‌تن is a compound adjective 'without body / disembodied' modifying دشت: 'the plain without bodies.' سر و یال is a coordinated nominal phrase 'heads and manes' that serves as the predicate complement; بود is the past copula 'was.' The word order places the modifier before the noun and then the predicate, typical of poetic description.
segments.s212-g110: Again همه 'all/every' is fronted; گوش 'ear' is the noun. پر here is the adjectival use 'full (of)' (i.e., پرِ زخم = 'full of wounds'), followed by زخم 'wound' and گوپال, which here appears as a name (proper noun) attributing the wounds (genitive/attributive relation). بود is the past copula. Grammatically this reads 'every ear was full of Gopāl's wounds.'
segments.s212-g111: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' شد is the past 'became' and takes رزم ترکان 'the Turks' battle' as its subject (رزم + genitive ترکان). برین گونه is a demonstrative/adverbial phrase: برین (an older/poetic form orthographically related to 'این') + گونه 'manner/way' = 'in this manner/so'; سخت is a predicate adjective 'severe/harsh.' Together: 'When the Turks' battle became so severe.'
segments.s212-g112: ندیدند is the past transitive verb 'they did not see' (negated by the prefix ن-). ایرانیان is the explicitly stated subject following the verb (verb–subject order for emphasis). روی بخت is the object: روی 'face' and بخت 'fortune/fate' in a genitive/attributive relation ('the face of fortune'). The clause means 'the Iranians did not see the face of fortune,' i.e. they had no luck.
segments.s212-g137: کنون is an adverbial temporal marker 'now' placed at the clause start. چون here functions as a subordinating conjunction meaning 'as/when' and introduces the temporal clause. رخ روز is a genitive-like compound 'the face (appearance) of the day'; شد is the past-tense/single third-person copular/verb 'became/turned', and تیره‌گون is a predicate adjective formed with the -گون suffix meaning 'dark-hued/turned dark', postpositive to the verb.
segments.s212-g138: همه here qualifies the following noun phrase and means 'whole/entire' (not 'everyone'); روی کشور = 'the surface/face of the country' (روی = surface/side, کشور = realm). چو is the comparative 'like/as' introducing a simile, and دریای خون is the noun phrase 'a sea of blood' where خون refers to bloodshed; the whole segment states that the country's surface resembled a sea of blood.
segments.s212-g139: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one/someone' (an impersonal 'one'); جای آرام is the object/phrase 'a quiet place' (جای = place, آرام = calm/quiet). باید is an impersonal modal 'must/ought to', placed before the main verb; گزید is the stem/past form used here as the lexical verb 'to choose' following باید (classical/poetic word order: modal + verb).
segments.s212-g140: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. تیره شب = 'the dark night' (تیره = dark/ominous, شب = night). خود is emphatic 'itself/the very' modifying شب. توان is the modal 'can' (ability), and آرمید is the verb 'to rest/sleep' (here used after توان to express the potential 'can rest'); together they form the protasis 'if (one) can rest in the dark night itself.'
segments.s212-g141: مگر here is used in the restrictive sense 'unless' (introducing the exception). کشته is a substantive 'the slain' (a killed person). یابد is an aorist/present-subjunctive-like form of یافتن 'to find' (third-person singular), so the clause reads 'unless the slain finds'. بجای مغاک is a prepositional phrase 'in place of the abyss' (بجای = instead of/in place of, مغاک = abyss).
segments.s212-g142: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/article 'a/one' introducing the items the slain might find. بستر = 'a bed'. از ریگ = 'made of/from sand' (از = of/from, ریگ = sand). و links the coordinated item چادر ز خاک = 'a tent of earth' (چادر = tent, ز poetic = 'of/from', خاک = earth/soil). The whole noun phrase is the object of یابد.
segments.s212-g143: همه is the subject 'all/everyone'. بازگشتند is the past-tense third-person plural verb 'they returned'. یکسر is an adverb meaning 'entirely/at once/straightaway' modifying the verb. ز جنگ is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from the battle'. The clause therefore states that everyone returned together from the fight.
segments.s212-g144: This is a coordinated ablative-result phrase describing their condition: ز خویشان introduces 'from/because of their kinsmen', modifying روان خسته = 'souls/spirits tired' (روان = spirit/soul, خسته = tired). و links the second coordinated phrase سر ز ننگ = 'their head(s) from shame' (سر = head, ز = from/of, ننگ = shame). Grammatically, the repeated 'ز' marks the source/cause for each affected noun (spirit and head).
segments.s212-g65: پدر را بگفت is an inverted, somewhat archaic way to say “he said to his father” (را here marks the definite/affected noun as recipient). بگفت is the past-tense verb with the old preformative b- (he said/told). آنچ introduces a reported content clause meaning “that which/what,” and جادو چه کرد is a subordinate clause ‘what the magic did’ with کرد as simple past.
segments.s212-g66: چه آورد is another content clause ‘what it brought/caused’; آورد here means ‘bring about/cause’. بر ما is a prepositional phrase ‘upon us’, and بروز نبرد is an adverbial phrase ‘on the day/occasion of battle’ (بروز = ‘on the day/occasion’, نبرد = ‘battle’). Word order is poetic: the object clause heads with چه and the place/time phrase follows.
segments.s212-g67: بدیدند is simple past plural ‘they saw’. ازان پس is a temporal phrase ‘after that/thereupon’. دلیران شاه is a genitive construction ‘the king’s brave (men)’ (in classical Persian the ezāfe is often omitted in writing), so the whole phrase means ‘after that the king’s brave men saw…’.
segments.s212-g68: چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’; دریای خون is a noun phrase ‘a sea of blood’ (دریای = sea + genitive خون). گشته is a perfect/participial form meaning ‘has become/turned into’, and آوردگاه ‘battlefield’ is the noun undergoing that change. Literally: ‘(the) battlefield has become like a sea of blood.’
segments.s212-g69: همه دشت is ‘the whole plain’ (همه = whole/entire; دشت = plain). کشته ز ایرانیان is a verbless/elliptical phrase meaning ‘slain of the Iranians’ (ز = poetic preposition ‘from/of’ marking source or agent), so the clause conveys ‘the whole plain [was] of the slain of the Iranians.’ The copula is omitted, as typical in epic diction.
segments.s212-g70: This is a parallel, elliptical apposition: تن بی‌سران = ‘bodies of the headless’ (تن = body; بی‌سران = headless ones) and سر بی‌تنان = ‘heads of the bodiless’ (سر = head; بی‌تنان = without bodies). The bi- prefix creates the ‘without–’ adjectives; the terse order is typical of descriptive lists in the epic.
segments.s212-g71: چنین گفت is a formulaic ‘thus said’. گودز (Goudarz) is the speaker’s name/agent. آنگه = ‘then’ and بطوس is the addressee (Batous) — the preposition به is omitted, as is common in longer epic speech formulas: ‘Thus Goudarz said then to Batous…’.
segments.s212-g72: که introduces the reported content clause ‘that…’. نه ... و نه is correlative negation ‘neither … nor …’. پیل ماند = ‘no elephant remained’ (ماند = remained/was left). آوای کوس = ‘the sound/beat of the war-drum’ (آوای = sound/voice; کوس = war-drum). Together: ‘that neither the elephant remained, nor the drum’s sound.’
segments.s212-g81: مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'. The clause orders 'تو' (you, resumed subject) not to take 'پیشدستی' (the precedence / initiative) 'در جنگ ما' (in/against our battle); word order is verb–object–subject marker typical of Persian imperatives with the prepositional phrase 'در جنگ ما' placed after the verbal phrase to mark the arena of action.
segments.s212-g82: کنند is a present/subjunctive/optative-form verb meaning 'they do / they make' (here a general future/characteristic action); the subject is این دلیران 'these brave ones'. خود functions as intensive/possessive 'their own' and combines with اهنگ 'purpose/intention' while ما is the object ('toward us' / 'against us'). The clause means roughly 'these brave men set their purpose toward us'.
segments.s212-g83: بپیش + زمانه form a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'toward the times/fortune'; پذیره مشو is a negative compound: پذیره (receptive/accepting) + مشو 'do not become'. Together the clause says 'do not become receptive to (or be taken in by) the times/fortune.'
segments.s212-g84: بنزدیک 'near/close at hand' sets the location; بدخواه 'ill-wisher' names the person you are near; خیره مشو is again negative 'do not become stupefied / astonished / transfixed'. The order is locative — noun — predicate, giving the admonition 'do not stand there amazed beside an enemy/ill-wisher.'
segments.s212-g85: This clause places 'تو' (you) and a locative 'در قلب' (in the heart) before 'با کاویانی درفش' (with the Kayanian banner), literally 'you (have) in the heart the Kayanian banner.' The preposition با links the banner as the thing held internally; word order emphasizes the image (heart before banner).
segments.s212-g86: همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle 'always / continually'; دار here functions as the verb 'hold/keep' addressed back to 'تو' (an archaic present-imperative/imperative-like use). در چنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in the grip/clutch of', and تیغ بنفش 'purple sword' names the instrument. Together: 'constantly keep (it) in the grip of the purple sword.' Note: the printed noun senses for دار do not match this verbal use, so it is an archaic verbal form.
segments.s212-g87: سوی میمنه is a directional phrase 'toward the right (hand)'; the two names 'گیو و بیژن' follow and then بهم ('together') closes the noun group. This is a straightforward directional listing: 'to the right, Giv and Bijan together...'.
segments.s212-g88: نگهبانش is 'نگهبان' (guard) with the enclitic -ش ('his/their/its'), so 'his guard' or 'their guard'; بر is the preposition 'on/upon'; میسره here names the place/state (an older word meaning something like 'available/at hand' or 'on that flank'); گستهم is an archaic past-first-person verb form meaning 'I placed / I set / I spread' — the speaker is saying they set those men as guards on that side. The sequence is object–preposition–location–verb (typical of older epic Persian).
segments.s212-g33: A nominal phrase listing participants and a temporal modifier: 'سپهدار و گردنکشان' = 'the commander and the proud ones' (coordinated nouns). 'آن زمان' is a temporal phrase 'at that time' placed after the coordinated noun phrase; in Persian the demonstrative 'آن' precedes 'زمان' to mark the time.
segments.s212-g34: Verb + object + directional phrase. 'گرفتند' is the past plural of گرفتن used idiomatically inchoatively ('they took up / began') with the noun 'زاری' (lamentation) as its object; 'سوی آسمان' is a prepositional/directional phrase 'toward the heaven/sky' indicating the direction of the lament.
segments.s212-g35: 'که‌ای' is an archaic/exclamatory vocative construction (equivalent to 'O you who...'); 'برتر از ...' is a comparative structure meaning 'superior to ...' and takes the following nouns 'دانش و هوش و رای' (a triad: knowledge, intellect, and counsel/judgment) as the standards of comparison.
segments.s212-g36: A triple negation using the correlative 'نه ... و نه ...' meaning 'neither ... nor ...'. Each short phrase is locative: 'در جای' (in/within a place or position), 'بر جای' (on/above a place or in a higher position), and 'زیر جای' (under/below a place). The repetition of 'جای' after different prepositions is normal in Persian for emphasis/variation of locus.
segments.s212-g37: 'همه' here is an intensifier meaning 'altogether/wholly'; it modifies the following noun phrase 'بنده پرگناه' ('sinful servant'). 'توایم' is an archaic poetical fusion meaning 'I am yours' (تو + ام), used as the copula/personal ending in classical Persian.
segments.s212-g38: 'به بیچارگی' is an adverbial phrase 'in/through my misery' (به + noun) indicating circumstance. 'دادخواه' is a nominal agent 'seeker of justice / petitioner' placed before the copula-like 'توایم' ('I am yours'), so the clause reads 'I am, in my misery, your seeker of justice' — the second occurrence of 'توایم' is the same archaic 'I am yours' form.
segments.s212-g39: 'ز' and 'از' are poetic prepositions meaning 'from' / 'because of' and introduce causes: 'ز افسون' = 'from/through enchantment' and 'از جادوی برتری' = 'from the magic/sorcery of superiority'; 'جادوی' is in ezāfe linking to 'برتری' ('the magic of superiority').
segments.s212-g40: Two coordinated nominal predicates describing the addressee by ellipsis of the copula: 'جهاندار' = 'lord/sovereign of the world', and 'بر داوران داوری' literally 'judgment over the judges' — i.e. one who judges the judges. 'داوری' is a verbal noun (judging/judgment) governed by the preposition 'بر' plus the plural 'داوران'.
segments.s212-g97: مرا مرگ نامی‌تر از سرزنش = 'For me, death is more famed/honorable than reproach.' Here مرا (mara) marks the experiencer/beneficiary ('to me' / 'for me') and is fronted for emphasis; مرگ is the subject ('death'); نامی‌تر is the comparative adjective 'more famous/more honorable' formed from نامی + تر; از introduces the standard of comparison ('than') and سرزنش is the noun 'reproach/condemnation'. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (object/beneficiary first).
segments.s212-g98: بهر جای بیغاره بدکنش = 'better than being disgraced anywhere by an ill-doer.' بهر جای ('for/at any place' or 'wherever') qualifies the domain in which the reproach would occur. بیغاره is an archaic/obscure word (see note) and بدکنش is a compound meaning 'evil‑doer' or 'one who behaves badly'; together they express being shamed or ill-treated by a wicked person in any place.
segments.s212-g99: چنین است گیتی پرآزار و درد = 'Thus is the world: full of hurt and pain.' چنین است is the copular phrase 'thus it is'; گیتی (world) is the grammatical subject placed after the copula for poetic word order; پرآزار is a prefixed adjective meaning 'full of harm/troublesome', and درد means 'pain/affliction'.
segments.s212-g100: ازو تا توان گرد بیشی مگرد = 'From it (the world), as long as you are able, do not go after more.' ازو ('from it') sets the source; تا توان = 'while/as long as you can' (تا + توان = temporal clause with توان as modal 'can'); گرد بیشی is a verbal phrase with گرد (go/turn) + بیشی (more) meaning 'pursue more/seek excess'; مگرد is a negative instruction/optative ('do not turn / do not become') — in effect a warning not to pursue more of the world's goods while you can avoid it.
segments.s212-g101: فزونیش یک روز بگزایدت = 'Its increase may grant you one day.' فزونیش = 'its increase/abundance' (فزون + -ِش genitive/possessive), یک روز = 'one day', and بگزایدت is an optative/verb form meaning 'may it grant/choose you' (ب‑ prefix + گزاید + ‑ت enclitic 'you'), expressing a wish/possibility that the world's increase gives you a single extra day.
segments.s212-g102: ببودن زمانی نیفزایدت = 'To being (i.e., to your existence) it will not add time.' ببودن is the infinitival/nominal form 'to be / being'; زمانی here refers to 'time / a period'; نیفزایدت is negative optative/indicative 'it will not add to you' (ن‑ prefix + افزاید + ‑ت enclitic 'you'), saying that even if wealth gives a day, it does not truly lengthen your life.
segments.s212-g103: دگر باره بر شد دم کرنای = 'Again (once more) the trumpet's breath/blast rose up.' دگر باره is the adverbial 'again'; بر شد is the past compound 'rose up / was raised' (بر + شد); دم کرنای is a genitive phrase 'the breath/blast of the trumpet' where دم = 'breath/blast' and کرنای = 'trumpet' (کرنا = trumpet, کرنای = its blast). The order is poetic (adverb + verbal phrase + nominal phrase).
segments.s212-g104: خروشیدن زنگ و هندی درای = 'The clamor/roar of bells and the (Indian) instruments sounded.' خروشیدن is an infinitive/gerund 'to roar / to resound' used as a verbal noun here; زنگ = 'bell/ringing'; و = 'and'; هندی modifies درای as 'Indian (whatever درای denotes)'. درای is an archaic/poetic word in this context; together the phrase names the clamor of bells and Indian instruments (i.e., the great noise of war instruments).
segments.s212-g153: برفتند is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural verb meaning “they went/left.” با شادمانی is a prepositional phrase meaning “with joy/rejoicing” (با + noun). زجای is the fused preposition ز (poetic from) + جای “place,” meaning “from their place(s).” Together this clause says who left, how (joyfully), and from where.
segments.s212-g154: نشستند is a past, 3rd‑person plural verb “they sat” (they took seats). The preposition بر here marks direction/position (toward/in front of) and is followed by پیش “before/in the presence of,” so بر پیش = “in front of / before.” پرده‌سرای names the place (a curtained chamber/stage); the whole short clause means “they sat before the curtain‑hall.” Note: the lemma given for نشستند in the data appears mismatched (it lists washing senses), so the actual verbal meaning is supplied in the gloss.
segments.s212-g155: همه شب is a temporal expression “all night” (همه = whole/entire + شب = night). ز is the poetic preposition “from/because of,” introducing the cause: ز آوای چنگ و رباب = “from the sound/melody of the harp and rebab.” Word order is typical Persian: temporal phrase, causal preposition + possessor phrase.
segments.s212-g156: سپه را نیامد is an impersonal/experiential construction where خواب (sleep) is the thing that 'did not come' to سپه (the army). را here marks the definite/affected noun سپه. نیامد is past negative “did not come.” بران دشت = بر + آن (contracted to بران = “on that/thereon”) + دشت “plain,” so بران دشت means “on that plain.” The phrase literally: “Sleep did not come to the army on that plain.”
segments.s212-g157: وزین روی is an adjectival phrase: وزین (weighty/dignified/grave) qualifies روی “face/visage,” so “with solemn/grave faces.” لشکر همه places the subject after this modifier: لشکر = army, همه “all/the whole” emphasizes totality. مستمند functions as a predicate/adjective “afflicted/needy,” so the clause describes the army’s condition. Word order: modifier phrase + subject + predicate.
segments.s212-g158: پدر بر پسر uses پدر “father” and the preposition بر (here meaning roughly “for/over/about”), marking the target of grief: ‘fathers for/over sons.’ پسر is the object of that emotion. سوگوار is an adjective “mourning,” and نژند is another adjective meaning “base/wretched/abject.” The two adjectives are joined by و; the clause portrays fathers as mourning and humbled/lowly. The preposition بر here indicates the person toward whom the grief is directed (a common idiom in classical Persian).
segments.s212-g159: همه دشت 'the whole plain' uses همه as an intensifier on دشت. پر + کشته means “full of slain” (پر as a possessive/fullness marker: “full (of)”), and و خسته adds “and exhausted/weary.” بود is the past copula “was.” So the clause states the plain’s condition in the past: “the whole plain was full of slain and of the weary.”
segments.s212-g160: بخون بزرگان زمین شسته بود reads as an agentive/passive phrase: شسته بود is past perfect/passive “had been washed.” بزرگان زمین “the great men of the land” is the genitive phrase supplying whose blood it was. بخون is the prepositional combination بِ + خون meaning “with/by blood” (written together here). Thus the clause means “it had been washed with the blood of the great men of the land.” Note: the tokenization in the data gives an imperative sense for بخون which does not fit the context, so the semantic index for that token is set to -1.
segments.s212-g185: خروشی (a cry/roar) is the direct noun describing the sound; برآورد here is a poetic past verb meaning ‘raised/uttered’ (older spelling برآورد), and آنگه is a temporal adverb ‘then’. بزار is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning ‘let/let loose’ or ‘caused to be’ and links the shout to what follows — together the clause says he gave/let out a cry and then (something happened). Word order is subjectless/impersonal: noun + verb + temporal adverb + causative verb.
segments.s212-g186: فراوان is an adverb/adjective ‘plentiful(ly)’ modifying the verb ببارید, a past tense verb ‘fell/ran (as rain)’ used metaphorically for blood. خون is the noun object and در کنار gives the location ‘at/in the edge/side’. The verb precedes the object (poetic flexibility) but the clause means ‘blood fell/plenty of blood poured at the side’.
segments.s212-g187: همی گفت is the habitual/continuous poetic construction meaning ‘he kept saying’ or ‘he was saying’. اگر begins a conditional clause ‘if’. نوذر پاک‌تن is a proper name with an epithet: نوذر (Nowzar) + پاک‌تن ‘pure-bodied’ (poetic adjective). The clause sets up a counterfactual/hypothetical condition.
segments.s212-g188: نکشتی here functions as the negative second-person past (یا imperfect) ‘you did not kill’ in a conditional sense (poetic spelling/wording); بن و بیخ من is a compound image ‘my root and trunk/rootstock’ (metaphorical for lineage or foundation), and بر چمن gives the location ‘on the meadow’. Word order places the conditional verb before the speaker’s metaphorical consequence.
segments.s212-g189: نبودی مرا is a negative past/conditional construction ‘there would not have been for me’ / ‘I would not have had’; مرا is the clitic object/experiencer ‘for me’. رنج و تیمار و درد is a triad of nouns ‘suffering, care, and pain’ that function as the things the speaker would not have experienced. The poetic order places the negated auxiliary first followed by the affected person and the list of afflictions.
segments.s212-g190: This continuation names the causes/kinds of those afflictions: غم ‘grief/sorrow’, کشته here denotes ‘the slain’ (people killed), and گرم دشت نبرد is a compound image ‘the heated/plain of battle’ (گرم = hot/fierce, دشت = plain, نبرد = battle). The nouns are juxtaposed to describe the scene that causes the speaker’s suffering.
segments.s212-g191: که تا is a connective phrase introducing an explanatory clause (‘for/since’); من is the subject ‘I’. کمر بر میان بسته‌ام is a compound participial/past construction: کمر here as ‘girdle/waist’, بر میان ‘in the middle/midst’ and بسته‌ام (بستن + -ام) is the perfect ‘I have bound/girded’. Together it expresses ‘I have girded my loins in the midst’ (prepared for action).
segments.s212-g192: بدل here means ‘in return / nevertheless’ or ‘on the other hand’; خسته‌ام‌گر is a concessive clause ‘though I am tired’ (گر introducing concession), بجان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘by/with (my) life’ and رسته‌ام (رستن) is the perfect ‘I have been saved/escaped’. Together: ‘though I am tired, I have been saved with my life.’
segments.s212-g177: ازان روزگاری is a temporal phrase meaning 'since that time' (poetic از + آن). کجا here is a rhetorical/relative 'where/wherever' qualifying زاده‌ام ('I was born'); word order is inverted for poetic effect (temporal phrase — rhetorical locative — verb). زاده‌ام is a past perfective/passive sense ('I was born') formed with the past participle plus the first-person enclitic.
segments.s212-g178: ز خفتان میان is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the midst of sleep' (ز = 'from', خفتان = 'sleep', میان = 'midst/middle'). هیچ نگشاده‌ام contains the negative prefix ن- (implicit in نگشاده‌ام) meaning 'I have not opened'; هیچ is an emphatic negator 'at all/never' here. نگشاده‌ام is the negative perfect participle construction 'I have not opened'.
segments.s212-g179: بفرجام is an adverbial phrase 'at the end / in the end'. چندین پسر is the noun phrase 'several sons'. ز انجمن ('from/of the assembly') identifies their origin or group; poetic texts often use ز for 'of/from'. The whole segment is an adverbial + noun phrase giving what will be seen.
segments.s212-g180: ببینم is the first-person present/subjunctive form of دیدن, used here narratively as 'I see' or 'I behold'. چنین کشته is a verbless verblike clause in which چنین ('thus/so') modifies کشته ('slain'); in Persian poetry participles like کشته stand as predicates or adjectives. در پیش من ('before me / in my presence') is a locative phrase completing the observation.
segments.s212-g181: جدا گشته is a compound verbal phrase: جدا (apart) + گشته (perfect participle/auxiliary) = 'has gone apart / has parted'. از من 'from me' marks the origin of separation. چو here is the simile marker 'as/like' and is followed by the name بهرام پور; the whole segment means 'he has parted from me like Bahram-pūr'.
segments.s212-g182: This is a string of adjectival and nominal epithets describing that person: چنان ('such/so'), نامور ('renowned'), شیر ('lion' — metaphor for bravery), خودکام ('autocratic/tyrannical'), پور ('son' as a name-element). Poetic Persian often stacks attributes like this; پور here repeats the patronymic element, appositive to the name in the previous segment.
segments.s212-g183: This is a temporal clause: ز گودرز ('from Goudarz') indicates source of the report, چون آگهی شد ('when (he) was informed/when the news came') uses چون as 'when' and شد as the auxiliary 'became/was', and بطوس (at the end) is the person affected. In natural English: 'When Batūs was informed by Goudarz'.
segments.s212-g184: مژه کرد is literally 'he made (his) eyelashes' and with the complement پر خون ('full of blood') means 'his eyelashes filled with blood' (کرد is causative/do). The conjunction و links رخ ('face') with سندروس (a fragrant wood/sandalwood) used as a color/attribute; the verb 'made' is understood to apply to both parts, a common ellipsis in Persian poetry (i.e. 'he made his eyelids full of blood and his face like sandalwood').
segments.s212-g129: که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the clause. ما را is the object 'us' with the postposed definite/object marker را. بدین گونه is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in this manner' formed from the preposition/particle ب + دین (here part of the frozen form) + گونه 'manner'. بگذاشتند is a past plural verb 'they left' (archaic/literary use of گذاشتن meaning 'to leave' here) agreeing with an understood plural agent.
segments.s212-g130: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' that links this clause to the previous one. روی 'face' is the noun that takes the action; از جنگ 'from the battle' uses the poetic preposition از. برگاشتند is a past plural verb 'they turned back/returned' (here 'turned away' in sense of withdrawing), agreeing with the same implied actors.
segments.s212-g131: برو is a 2nd-person singular imperative 'go'. بازگردان is another imperative (2nd-sing.) meaning 'bring back / turn back / restore' and is coordinated with برو as consecutive commands. سپه را is the object 'the army' marked by را. ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road/route' (ز = poetic 'from', راه = 'road, way').
segments.s212-g132: ز here gives a causal/benefactive sense 'because of / for', introducing reasons. بیغاره is an archaic/obscure word (not transparent in the provided senses) and functions as part of the reason clause; دشمن 'enemy' and شرم شاه 'the king's shame' are coordinated by و 'and', with شرم 'shame' governed by شاه 'king' (the shame pertaining to the king).
segments.s212-g133: بشد is an archaic narrative particle/verb meaning roughly 'it happened/then' (here used to set the scene). گیو و لشکر همه is the coordinated subject 'Giv and the entire army'. بازگشت is here a past verb 'returned' agreeing with that plural subject (the form is literary and the verb appears after the subject in this poetic order).
segments.s212-g134: پر از کشته is an adjectival phrase 'full of slain' (پر 'full' + از 'of' + کشته 'the slain'). دیدند is the past plural verb 'they saw'. هامون و دشت are the locative/visual objects 'plain and field' (what they saw was that the plain and field were full of the slain); poetic word order places the verb before these nouns.
segments.s212-g135: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed / general'. چنین گفت is a stock phrase 'thus said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت here used as a past verb 'said' in literary register). با مهتران is a prepositional/indirect object phrase 'to/with the chiefs/commanders' indicating the addressees; مهتران is a plural honorific for high officers. Note: some lemmas provided are mismatched to the literal use, so the verb and مهتران are literary uses.
segments.s212-g136: که introduces reported speech or a content clause 'that'. اینست is the present/near demonstrative copula 'this is'. پیکار جنگ‌آوران is a noun phrase 'the battle (pikar) of the warriors' where جنگ‌آوران ('warriors') modifies or is in genitive relation to پیکار; the clause is the spahbed's statement identifying the scene as a warriors' battle.
segments.s212-g25: در و دشت is a coordinate nominal phrase 'houses/doorways and plains' (در here = settlement/door/house in older usage). گشته is the perfect/participle of گشتن meaning 'have become'; پر از X is the idiom 'full of X', so گشته پر از برف و خون = 'have become full of snow and blood'. Word order is topical: place phrase first, then the resulting state.
segments.s212-g26: This is a short nominal clause: سواران ایران = 'the horsemen of Iran' (possessive/genitive). فتاده is a perfect participle meaning 'fallen', and نگون is an adjective 'ill‑fated/overthrown' modifying them. The verb 'are' is omitted (typical poetic verbless predication).
segments.s212-g27: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of/from'. 'کشته' here refers to the slain (noun). نبد (نبود) is the negative existential 'there was not'; جای رفتن بجنگ is a noun phrase 'a place to go to the battle' where رفتن (infinitive) governs بجنگ ('to fight/into battle'). The whole means 'there was no place left to go into battle because of the slain.'
segments.s212-g28: Repeated ز marks causes: 'from the snow and from the scattered (bodies)'. افگنده is an adjective/participle 'scattered/thrown (about)' modifying an implicit referent (the fallen). شد is 'became', and جای تنگ = 'space/room (became) narrow' — i.e. there was little room to move. Poetic order places the verb early.
segments.s212-g29: سیه گشت = 'turned/grew dark' (گشت as 'became'); در دشت sets the location 'in the plain'. شمشیر و دست are coordinated nouns 'swords and hands/power' — lit. 'swords and the hand' — meaning weapons and strength are stained/darkened (with blood). The verb precedes its objects for poetic effect.
segments.s212-g30: بروی is an imperative/hortative form from رفتن 'go' (older poetic usage: 'go!'), اندر افتاده = 'have fallen inside/within' (an intransitive perfect participle), and برسان مست is an instruction 'bring/relay (the news) to the intoxicated (one)'. The verb برسان here probably means 'inform/bring word to'; the direct object (the news) is implied.
segments.s212-g31: نبد (نبود) is the archaic negative 'there was not'. جای گردش = 'place/space for turning/maneuver' (گردش here = 'turning, movement'). دران رزمگاه = 'in that battlefield' (دران = 'in that / within it'). Together: 'There was no room to maneuver in that battlefield.'
segments.s212-g32: شده is the perfect/passive auxiliary 'has become'; دست لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the hand/strength of the army' (دست = power/control). ز سرما gives cause 'because of/by the cold', and تباه is the adjective 'ruined/wasted'. Word order places شده before its predicate for poetic rhythm: 'the army's strength has become ruined by the cold.'
segments.s212-g161: This is a noun phrase describing location and body parts: چپ و راست (literally “left and right”) gives the two sides/directions; آوردگه is an older/poetic noun meaning “place of arrival/where one comes to” and here heads the phrase; دست و پای (“hands and feet”) are coordinated objects that specify what is to be placed. Word order is typical poetic Persian where modifiers and coordinates appear before or around the head noun.
segments.s212-g162: نهادن is the infinitive “to put/place” and is followed by the main past-tense verb ندانست (“did not know”); classical Persian often places the infinitive before the finite verb. کس is the indefinite subject/object meaning “anyone/no one” (here rendering negative: “no one”); پا بجای is an adverbial phrase “foot in (its) place” = “where to set a foot.” Together: “no one knew how to place hand or foot.”
segments.s212-g163: همه شب همی is a temporal adverbial—'all night' with همی marking continued/habitual action; خسته is the object (the exhausted/wounded) and برداشتند is the past plural verb 'they took/lifted/carried off.' The subject is omitted (understood from context) and the clause describes a continuous nocturnal action.
segments.s212-g164: چو here introduces a simile/condition 'as if' or 'as when'; بیگانه بد خوار is a nominal group 'a stranger, mean/despising (one)' acting as subject of the following verb; بگذاشتند is past plural 'they left/abandoned.' The clause reads like a comparison: 'as if a mean stranger had left (them),' with چو marking the simile.
segments.s212-g165: بر خسته is a locative prepositional phrase 'over/near the exhausted (ones)'; آتش همی سوختند literally places the noun 'fire' with the past plural verb 'they kept burning' (همی indicates continuing action). The implied agents (they) keep the fire burning beside the wounded/exhausted.
segments.s212-g166: گسسته is a participial adjective 'torn/broken' acting as the object; two coordinated past verbs follow: ببستند 'they bound/tied' and بردوختند 'they sewed' (connected by و). The sequence describes first binding the torn parts and then sewing them—both past plural actions by the same implied agents.
segments.s212-g167: فراوان is a quantifier 'many/plentiful'; ز is the poetic genitive 'of/from' linking فراوان to گودرزیان 'the Gudarzis' (followers/men of Gudarz). خسته بود is the predicate 'were exhausted' with بود as the past copula. The clause states that a large number among Gudarz's men were exhausted.
segments.s212-g168: This is two short parallel clauses joined by و. بسی is an intensifier/quantifier 'many' (here used before predicates); کشته بود 'had been killed/there were many killed' and بسته بود 'had been bound/there were many bound' use بود as past verbal copula/auxiliary. The structure reports tallies: many slain and many bound.
segments.s212-g73: همه یکسره تیغها برکشیم is an exhortatory clause with the adverbial phrase همه یکسره (‘all/together, wholly’) fronted for emphasis, followed by the (definite) object تیغها (‘the swords’). برکشیم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/optative used to mean “let us draw/raise” (a common poetic imperative). Word order is adverbial + object + verb, typical in elevated/poetic Persian.
segments.s212-g74: This small clause contains an address and a conditional pair. براریم here functions as an address/verb meaning roughly ‘let us raise’ or an invocation to brethren (the lemma given is ‘brother/peer’ and is used vocatively or collectively), with جوش (‘ardor, surge of passion’) as its object. The two ار...ار construction is archaic conditional: ار کشند (if they draw) + ار کشیم (if/we will draw). کشند is 3rd-person plural and کشیم is 1st-person plural (subjunctive/optative), both used in the protasis/apodosis pattern.
segments.s212-g75: همانا که is an emphatic introductory particle (‘verily/indeed that’). ما را uses the 1st-person plural with the object marker را (here marking the affected party), and سر آمد is a compound where سر (literally ‘head’) combines with آمد (‘came’) to mean ‘the time has come’ or ‘the appointed moment has arrived.’ زمان is the noun ‘time/season’ completing the clause; آمد is past 3rd-person singular.
segments.s212-g76: نه negates the following noun phrase: روز نبردست و تیر و کمان = ‘a day of battle and of bow and arrow.’ نبردست is an older adjective/compound meaning ‘for battle’ or ‘battle-worthy’; it qualifies روز. The coordinating و links تیر (‘arrow’) and کمان (‘bow’). The whole unit is a negated predicate: ‘not a day for fighting/archery.’
segments.s212-g77: بدو is the dative/enclitic meaning ‘to him/unto him’ (it introduces the indirect object of گفت). گفت here is the verb ‘said’ in past tense (3rd-person), and طوس‌ای is vocative ‘O Tūs’ or ‘Tūs, O…’ with the vocative suffix -ای. جهاندیده مرد is an appositive vocative (‘worldly-wise man’ or ‘sage’). Word order is dative + verb + vocative phrase, a compact poetic order.
segments.s212-g78: هوا گشت پاک is a simple clause: هوا (‘air/mood/atmosphere’) is subject and گشت (past of گشتن) means ‘became/turned,’ so گشت پاک = ‘became clear/pure.’ The conjunction و links the next clause; بشد باد سرد uses بشد (past-form/poetic optative meaning ‘it became/there came to be’) plus باد سرد (‘a cold wind’), so ‘a cold wind arose/blew.’
segments.s212-g79: چرا is the interrogative ‘why’. سر همی داد is an idiomatic sequence: سر (‘head’/life) + همی (an old habitual/intensifying particle) + داد (past of دادن ‘gave’) — here expressing the idea of ‘giving up one’s head/life.’ باید is the modal ‘must/ought’, and بباد (a passive/impersonal verbal form) means ‘be blown away/be lost.’ The clause questions giving up one’s head to be blown away; the order and particles are archaic/poetic.
segments.s212-g80: چو is a subordinating particle meaning ‘when’/‘since’. فریادرس is the subject (‘the rescuer’ or ‘one who comes to help’). فره و زور are coordinated objects/benefits (‘glory’ and ‘strength’ respectively). داد is the past-tense verb ‘gave’. Together this subordinate clause supplies the occasion or reason: ‘when a rescuer gives glory and strength.’
segments.s212-g1: ز is the poetic form of از and here marks inclusion/origin: 'among/from the Turks'. یکی بود is an existential past—'there was one' or 'a certain one existed'. بازور is an adjectival/adverbial epithet meaning 'by force' or 'powerfully' and modifies that one; نام at the end functions as 'by name' and introduces the following proper name in the next segment.
segments.s212-g2: بافسوس here is the personal name (called Bafsus). بهر introduces purpose/aim ('for' / 'toward'), followed by the noun phrase جای گسترده کام — 'a place (جای) broad/extended (گسترده) of desire (کام)'. The phrase as a whole expresses that he sought wide territories to satisfy his desires.
segments.s212-g3: بیاموخته functions here as a past participial/verbal phrase meaning 'having learned' or 'was taught' (an older/poetic usage; not the modern negative بی-). It takes the coordinated objects کژی و جادوی — 'crookedness/wrongdoing and magic/sorcery' — as the skills or vices he had acquired.
segments.s212-g4: بدانسته is a participial/verb form meaning 'knowing' or 'having known' and introduces the next items as things he knows. چینی and پهلوی are languages/ethnic-linguistic names; و هم is the coordinating 'and also' (هم emphasizes inclusion). Grammatically this is a verbless clause in English but in Persian the participle links his knowledge of those tongues to the previous statement about what he had learned.
segments.s212-g5: چنین گفت پیران is an inverted order meaning 'thus said the elders' (چنین = 'thus'; گفت is the speech verb). بافسون is the addressee (the name Bafsus) and پژوه is an imperative/infinitival verb 'seek' or 'search' used as a command; together they form a direct address plus command: 'Bafsus, seek/search.'
segments.s212-g6: کز introduces a relative/subordinate clause here meaning 'that/from which' → 'who/that', but functionally 'that/because of which' or simply 'that'. ایدر is an archaic form of 'here'. برو is an imperative 'go'. تا with a following place phrase expresses extent 'as far as' or 'to' (تا سر تیغ کوه = 'to the head/edge of the mountain's blade' i.e. the mountain ridge or summit). سر تیغ is a compound image 'head of the blade' = ridge/peak.
segments.s212-g7: یکی here works as 'one'/'a certain' introducing an itemized list: 'snow and cold and wind...'. The three weather nouns are coordinated by و. دمان (here) refers to the foothill/hem area (the base or skirt of the mountain), so the phrase groups 'snow, cold, and the wind of the foothills.'
segments.s212-g8: بریشان is a contracted form meaning بر ایشان 'upon them' (بر + the pronominal suffix -شان), so the verb بیاور is an imperative 'bring' with that object: 'bring upon them'. هم here emphasizes 'also/indeed', and اندر زمان means 'at the same time' or 'within the same moment' — together giving the sense 'bring upon them... all at once.'
segments.s212-g201: بدین here is an adverbial prepositional compound meaning 'for this' or 'to this end' (classical/poetic usage); من is the emphatic subject 'I' placed after the fronted adverbial; سواری is the direct object 'a horseman/ rider'; فرستاده‌ام is the perfect (past participle + auxiliary) meaning 'I have sent' (1st person singular). The word order is adverbial phrase → subject (emphatic) → object → verb.
segments.s212-g202: ورا is the clitic object 'to him' (refers back to the addressee); پیش ازین is the fixed temporal phrase 'before this / previously' (پیش + از + این split in the tokenization); آگهی داده‌ام is a compound verb phrase 'I have given notice / informed' (داده‌ام = 'I have given'), so the whole clause means 'I have already informed him of this.'
segments.s212-g203: مگر here introduces a negative condition 'unless' (classical usage). رستم is the subject (proper name). زال را is the direct object with the postposition را marking definiteness/emphasis. با سپاه is an instrumental/associative prepositional phrase 'with an army' that qualifies the sending.
segments.s212-g204: سوی ما is a directional phrase 'toward us'; فرستد is a 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/jussive form of 'send' used here in the subordinate conditional sense ('sends' or 'should send' after مگر); بدین رزمگاه is a locative/directional phrase 'to this battlefield' (بدین = 'to/for this' + رزمگاه 'battlefield'), placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s212-g205: وگرنه means 'otherwise'. ز ما is the poetic form of از ما 'of/from us' (i.e., 'among us'). نامداری is a noun used adjectivally 'renown/noted' modifying دلیر 'brave'; the noun phrase reads 'a renowned brave (man) of ours.'
segments.s212-g206: نماند is the negative present/future sense 'will not remain / will not be left' (3rd singular). به آوردگه is a locative phrase 'at the place/venue of contest' (به = 'at/to', آوردگه = 'venue/place of coming together'). بر چو شیر is a short simile: چو = 'like', شیر = 'lion', with بر functioning here as a prepositional/particle linking the simile to the verb phrase — 'to remain ... to (act) like a lion.'
segments.s212-g41: تو (addressing 'you' in the poetic register) is the subject; باشی is the present/subjunctive form used like an optative/assertive 'be/are' here (not a bare imperative). به بیچارگی is a prepositional phrase 'in/for helplessness' that marks the sphere in which the subject acts, and دستگیر is a nominal predicate 'helper, succorer' describing the role of 'you'.
segments.s212-g42: تواناتر is the comparative form of توانا, 'more powerful/strong'; از introduces the standard of comparison ('than'). آتش و زمهریر are the two items being compared ('fire and bitter cold'), joined by و. The phrase modifies or comments on the preceding predicate, asserting the subject's superiority to these forces.
segments.s212-g43: ازین (از + این) 'from/against this' introduces the obstacle ('this snow and cold'); برف و سرما are coordinated nouns. تو is the emphatic/subject pronoun, and فریادرس is a noun 'rescuer/succour' predicated of تو — the clause states that, in the face of the snow and cold, you are the rescuer.
segments.s212-g44: نداریم is the negative present plural 'we do not have'; the following فریادرس is the direct object ('a rescuer'). جز تو کس is an exceptive construction 'no one except you' (literally 'except you person'), so the whole clause means 'we have no rescuer besides you.'
segments.s212-g45: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (simple narrative). یکی مرد دانش‌پژوه is a nominal group 'a certain/one man, a scholar' where یکی functions like 'a certain' or 'one' and دانشن‌پژوه qualifies مرد as 'learned/scholarly.'
segments.s212-g46: برهام here functions as a dative 'to Bahram' (بر = 'toward/to' attached to the name); بنمود is the past 'showed' (بنمود = 'he displayed/shown'). آن تیغ کوه is the object 'that sword of the mountain' (آن = 'that', تیغ = 'sword', کوه = 'mountain'). The verb thus links the scholar's action to the recipient and the shown object.
segments.s212-g47: کجا is the interrogative 'where'; جای is 'place/spot' (the noun being queried). بازور is an adverbial ('by force' / 'with force'), and نستوه بود is the predicate 'was (n)estoo(h)' — an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'not able to withstand / distressed' here, so the clause asks rhetorically whether any place could stand against force.
segments.s212-g48: بر + افسون و تنبل = 'upon/over (it) were enchantment (افسون) and lethargy/sloth (تنبل)'; بران کوه بود places these qualities 'on that mountain' (بران = 'thereon/that'), and بود is the past copula 'was.' The clause states that the mountain was characterized by spells and sluggishness.
segments.s212-g113: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle here marking a realized or continuing action (‘then/so’). تیره شد is a past-tense verbal phrase: تیره (adjective 'dark/ominous') + شد (past of شدن, 'became/turned'), so تیره شد = 'became dark'. روی اختر درشت is a noun phrase where روی 'face' governs اختر 'star' (a poetic way to say 'the face of the star') with درشت as a post-nominal adjective 'large/bright'; Persian permits adjectives after the noun in poetry.
segments.s212-g114: دلیران is the plural subject 'the brave ones'. نمودند is the simple past 'they showed/did show'. پشت ('back') is placed after the verb as the direct object; بدشمن precedes it and qualifies the object as against/with respect to the enemy (i.e. 'their backs to the hostile [men]'). The whole clause is idiomatic: 'showed their backs' = 'turned their backs/fled'.
segments.s212-g115: This is an enumerative nominal group introduced by repeated چو 'like/as' (here functioning as a poetic marker for listing names). Each proper name follows چو; دلیر is a post-nominal adjective attached to گیو ('Giv the brave'). No finite verb appears in this fragment — it is a list of persons with epithets.
segments.s212-g116: Another part of the name-list using چو before names (چو = 'as/like'). شیدوش and بیژن are names here (although the lemma sense list gives a common-verb sense, in context they function as proper names); چو رهام شیر places the epithet شیر 'lion' after رهام (post-nominal epithet), a typical poetic device to liken a warrior to a lion.
segments.s212-g117: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject. برنهادند is the past plural of برنهادن (بر‑ prefix + نهادن) meaning 'set forth/laid out' in a transitive sense. جان را is the definite object (را marks it), and بکف is an adverbial phrase 'into the hand / in hand' — together an idiom meaning 'placed their lives in their hands' (i.e., risked their lives).
segments.s212-g118: همه again is subject. رزم جستند is a verbal phrase where جستند (past of پریدن/جستن 'they leapt/sprang') takes رزم 'battle' as its scope — idiomatically 'leapt into battle'. بر پیش صف is a directional prepositional sequence (بر = toward/on, پیش = front, صف = line/formation) meaning 'to the front of the battle line'.
segments.s212-g119: هرآنکس که ... بود is a standard relative clause: هرآنکس 'whoever/anyone', که introduces the clause, and بود is the past copula 'was'. با طوس 'with Tūṣ' is a prepositional phrase placing the person alongside Tūṣ; در جنگ specifies the setting 'in the battle'. Word order follows classical Persian: relative pronoun + predicate + copula at the end.
segments.s212-g120: همه is predicative here ('all/every one'). نامدار and کنارنگ are coordinated predicate adjectives joined by و, and بود is the past copula 'was' placed at the end as usual in Persian nominal sentences. Together they say that those people 'were renowned and [epithet]'. (کنارنگ is an epithet; its precise connotation is poetic/archaic and functions as a second predicate adjective.)
segments.s212-g145: سر از کوه برزد همانگاه ماه is a compact poetic clause: سر (‘head’) is the subject image (literally “the head”), از کوه is a prepositional phrase ‘from/out of the mountain’, and برزد is an archaic past/punctual verb meaning ‘came forth/rose’. همانگاه (‘at that same moment’) is an adverbial time placed after the verb for emphasis, and ماه (‘the moon’) is the referent of سر. Word order is flexible and poetic: image + source + verb + time + identity.
segments.s212-g146: چو بر تخت پیروزه، پیروز شاه: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction ‘when/as’. بر تخت is the prepositional phrase ‘upon the throne’, پیروزه is an adjectival/epithet modifying تخت (‘turquoise’/inlaid with turquoise), and پیروز شاه is the noun phrase ‘the victorious king’ (adjective before noun, poetic order). The comma-like pause is typical of Ferdowsi’s verse; the sense is temporal simultaneity (‘when the king was on his turquoise throne’).
segments.s212-g147: سپهدار پیران سپه را بخواند: سپهدار is the subject ‘the commander’; پیران سپه is a genitive/grouping ‘the elders of the army’ (poetic omission of an explicit ezâfe is common); را marks that phrase as the direct object; بخواند is a past-tense verb here meaning ‘called/summoned’ (bakhānd used with sense ‘to call’). Order is S O V in this line for metrical/poetic effect but functions as ‘the commander summoned the army’s elders’.
segments.s212-g148: همی گفت زیشان فراوان نماند: همی is a narrative/adverbial particle common in classical Persian indicating durative or emphatic past (‘kept/was saying’ or simply ‘then said’). گفت here is the reported-speech verb ‘said’. زیشان (‘of/from them’) identifies the group being spoken about; فراوان is the quantity adjective ‘many/much’; نماند is the negative past of ماندن ‘to remain’ meaning ‘did not remain/are no longer left’. The clause thus reports the commander’s remark that many of them were gone.
segments.s212-g149: بدانگه که دریای یاقوت زرد: بدانگه (‘thereupon/at that time’) is a temporal adverb introducing a new image; که is the subordinating/introducing particle linking the image to what follows; دریای is the possessive form ‘sea of…’; یاقوت here is used poetically (ruby/ruby-red) as the modifier of دریای; زرد is a color adjective ‘yellow’ placed after the noun cluster for metrical/poetic effect (adjective position is flexible in poetry).
segments.s212-g150: زند موج بر کشور لاژورد: موج is the subject/object ‘wave’; بر کشور is the prepositional phrase ‘over/upon the realm/land’; لاژورد (‘lapis’) names that realm (a poetic color/metaphor). زند here is a poetic present/past-form of زدن meaning ‘strikes/sends/lashes’ (a verbal action), and the verb governs موج as what is cast upon کشور. Note: زند is a terse archaic/poetic form; the clause reads ‘(it) sent/struck a wave upon the lapis land’.
segments.s212-g151: کسی را که زنده‌ست بیجان کنیم: کسی را is the indefinite object ‘anyone’ marked by را; که زنده‌ست is a relative clause ‘who is alive’ modifying کسی; بیجان is an object/complement meaning ‘lifeless/dead’ and کنيم is the subjunctive/imperative-plural form ‘let us make’ or ‘we will make’. The construction is causative: ‘we will make anyone who is alive (be) lifeless’. The order (object + relative clause + complement + verb) reflects poetic compression.
segments.s212-g152: بریشان دل شاه پیچان کنیم: بریشان is the clitic/prepositional cluster بر + ایشان ‘upon/for them’; دل شاه is a possessed noun phrase ‘the king’s heart’; پیچان is a causative/adjective meaning ‘causing to turn/twist’ (here used with کنيم to mean ‘we will cause to turn/afflict’); کنيم is the same subjunctive/optative verb ‘let us make/we will make’. Grammatically this is a causative clause: ‘we will cause the king’s heart to turn/twist over them’ (i.e. cause the king’s heart to be affected regarding them).
segments.s212-g193: هم‌اکنون is a temporal adverb placed at the front ('right now'). تن کشتگان را is a noun phrase with the definite object marker را after it (object = 'the bodies of the slain'); بخاک is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to/into the earth' placed before the verb — the verb itself appears in the next segment.
segments.s212-g194: بپوشید is a 2nd-person plural/formal imperative of پوشیدن used here in the sense 'cover' (so: 'bury' when combined with بخاک). جایی که باشد مغاک is a relative/adverbial clause: جایی 'somewhere/where', که 'that/where', باشد is 3rd-person (subjunctive/hortative-like) 'there be' used to mean 'where there is', and مغاک 'a chasm/abyss'.
segments.s212-g195: سران بریده is a noun (سران 'heads') modified by the past-participle adjective بریده 'severed/cut off'. سوی is the preposition 'toward' and تن is 'the body/bodies' placed before the verb; برید here functions as a verbal command/causative-like verb from بردن 'take/carry' in poetic usage ('bring/carry to'). Word order (object or goal before verb) is typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s212-g196: بنه is a noun 'bundle/heap' (here meaning a pile of corpses or remains) and is the theme to be moved; سوی کوه هماون is a double locative ('toward the mountain, that Homaun'), where هماون is a demonstrative/name specifying the mountain. برید repeats the verb 'carry/bring' in the same imperative/poetic sense as above.
segments.s212-g197: برانیم is 1st-person-plural form of راندن (here used narratively as 'we drove/led away' or hortatively 'let us drive'); لشکر 'army' is the direct object, همه 'all/the whole' qualifies it, and همگروه 'fellow-members/companions' further describes the group. The verb-before-object order reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.s212-g198: سراپرده و خیمه are coordinated objects ('the inner chambers and the tents'). بر سوی کوه is a locative phrase 'toward the mountain' where بر + سوی gives a strong directional sense; کوه is 'mountain'. The phrase indicates the destination to which the forces, chambers and tents are driven.
segments.s212-g199: هیونی (Hiun) is here the person/agent being sent; فرستیم is 1st-person-plural past/historical 'we sent'. نزدیک شاه is a locative/adverbial phrase 'near the king' or 'to the king's presence', i.e. indicating where Hiun was sent.
segments.s212-g200: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش). برفروزد is a 3rd-person form meaning 'is kindled / is set afire' (poetic use meaning 'was stirred/angered'), and فرستد سپاه is a 3rd-person verb + object 'he sent an army' (historical present/past narrative sequence: his heart kindled, and he sent troops).
segments.s212-g17: بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning 'ordered/commanded' with its subject omitted (typical in epic narrative). پیران here is the direct object (the person being ordered) — in the Shahnameh this is the proper name Piran (none of the supplied senses match exactly). که introduces the reported clause that follows. یکسر سپاه is a noun phrase meaning 'the army as a single whole' (یکسر = 'entire/one-piece' modifying سپاه = 'army').
segments.s212-g18: This is the content of the reported order. یکی here means 'one/single' (one single assault), حمله is 'attack', and سازید is an older/formal present/jussive form 'to make/perform' used here like 'should make' or an imperative in reported speech. زین is problematic in the given sense list (here it functions as 'from this' or 'out of this' in classical usage, not 'saddle'), so its appropriate sense is not provided. رزمگاه is 'battlefield' and completes the phrase 'from this battlefield.'
segments.s212-g19: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'. The repeated بر + noun sequence (بر نیزه بر دستهاشان) is a poetic placement meaning 'with spears on their hands' (بر = 'on', نیزه = 'spear', دستهاشان = 'their hands' where -شان is the possessive enclitic). فسرد is an obscure/poetic verb form here; none of the provided senses fit this usage, so the exact lexical sense is uncertain in the supplied list.
segments.s212-g20: نیاراست is an adjective/adverb meaning 'unsuitable' or 'ungainly' modifying the ensuing action. بنمود is past 'showed/manifested' (here 'made' or 'displayed' a movement). کس is an indefinite 'someone', and دست برد is a light-verb construction meaning 'put forth a hand / reached out' (دست = 'hand', برد here is part of the verbal phrase 'دست بردن' = 'to lay hands / to reach out'); the exact lexical sense for برد is not in the provided senses, so it is marked absent.
segments.s212-g21: وزان پس is a temporal sequence 'and/from that (then) after' (وزان = 'from that' or 'and from that', پس = 'after/then'). برآورد here is a literary verb meaning 'brought forth/raised' (used with غریو to mean 'uttered/raised a cry'). هومان is a proper name (Hooman). غریو is 'shout/clamor' and is the object of برآورد.
segments.s212-g22: یکی حمله آورد is another light-verb phrase: یکی = 'one/single' (one attack), حمله = 'attack', آورد = 'brought/launched' (here 'he launched an attack'). برسان in the supplied list does not match the prepositional/locative function it has in context (roughly 'upon/at'); its appropriate sense is not provided. دیو is 'demon' and is the target of the attack.
segments.s212-g23: بکشتند is a past plural verb 'they killed'. چندان here quantifies the extent 'so many / to such an extent'. ز is the classical preposition 'of/from' linking the following noun phrase; ایران سپاه = 'the army/host of Iran' (ایران here refers to the Iranian people, rather than just the land). Word order is the older poetic order (quantifier before the genitive).
segments.s212-g24: که introduces a result clause 'so that/that'. دریای is a possessive/genitive form 'sea of …' used metaphorically. خون here means 'blood' in the sense of 'bloodshed' (the second sense). گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned into' and takes آوردگاه as the complement: آوردگاه = 'battlefield' (literally 'place of bringing' but used here as 'the place of fighting'). Together: 'that the battlefield became a sea of blood.'
segments.s212-g49: بجنبید is a narrative past verb meaning 'moved/strede' (the b- prefix is an older/poetic verbal formation); here it describes Roham's action and takes رهام as its subject. زان is the compound 'از + آن' meaning 'from that', and رزمگاه is the place 'battlefield'; word order (verb + subject + place) is characteristic of epic Persian.
segments.s212-g50: برون is an adverb 'out/forth' modifying the verb تاخت (past of تاختن 'to gallop/rush'); اسپ is the subject placed after the verb (an archaic/poetic word order). از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'out of the midst of the army' describing the source of the steed's rush.
segments.s212-g51: زره دامنش را is a noun phrase with the possessive -ش 'his' (دامنش = 'its/ his hem, lower skirt of the garment/armor'); را marks that phrase as the direct object. بزد is the past tense 'he struck/struck together /fastened' and بر کمر is a locative 'upon the waist' — together implying he adjusted or struck the armored skirt against his belt/waist.
segments.s212-g52: پیاده here means 'on foot' (he dismounted or went on foot) and is followed by برآمد 'rose/ascended' (past tense). بران is a contracted locative ('بر + آن') meaning 'onto that' or 'thereon', and کوه سر is 'mountain-top/summit' (کوه + سر), so the clause says he went on foot up onto the summit of that mountain.
segments.s212-g53: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; جادو is 'the enchanter/enchantment' and بدیدش is بدید + clitic -ش 'he saw him/it'. بیامد is the past 'he came' and بجنگ ('to/for fighting') indicates purpose — 'he came to fight.' Note the clitic object on بدید and the infinitival purpose phrase بجنگ.
segments.s212-g54: عمودی is used as a noun-like form 'an upright [shaft/pike]' or 'upright weapon' (here functioning as the weapon's shape). ز is the poetic/genitive 'of/from', پولاد is 'steel' and چینی qualifies it 'Chinese (steel)'; بچنگ is a locative/adverbial 'into the grasp / in hand', so the whole phrase means 'with an upright (weapon) of Chinese steel in his hand.'
segments.s212-g55: چو is 'when'; رهام is the proper name subject; نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near' and جادو is the enchanter; رسید is the past tense 'reached/arrived' — together: 'When Roham came near the enchanter.'
segments.s212-g56: سبک modifies تیغ (a light-weight blade), and تیز further qualifies it 'sharp'; از میان is the prepositional 'from among/from the midst of' and برکشید is past 'he drew up/raised' — here used for drawing a weapon: 'he drew a light, sharp sword from among [his gear/in the midst].' Note سبک is adjectival before تیغ and برکشید is the action verb.
segments.s212-g89: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when” introducing a subordinate clause. رهام و شیدوش are coordinated subjects (X و Y = “Roham and Shidūsh”). بر + پیش + صف is a prepositional phrase: بر (toward) + پیش (the front/before) + صف (the battle line) = “toward the front of the line.”
segments.s212-g90: This clause describes their action and result. گرازه here evokes a boar-like image (literally a boar/young boar) and بکین is a poetic/dialectal form meaning “they did/made” (cf. بکنند); together they mean “they snorted/ran like boars.” برلب آورده is a perfect participle phrase (“having brought to the lip”), with آورده “brought” and کف “foam” as the resulting state: “foam brought to their lips.”
segments.s212-g91: شوم is optative/jussive “may I / let me” (a wish or oath). برکشم is the verb ‘lift/draw’ here (the provided lemma senses do not match this verbal sense, so the correct lexical sense is not in the given list). گرز کین is the object: گرز “mace” + کین “of vengeance” = “the mace of vengeance.” از میان is a prepositional phrase “from among / out of (their midst).”
segments.s212-g92: کنم is the first‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here as the main verb “I will/do.” تن فدی is an object phrase meaning “my body as a ransom/sacrifice” (تن = body; فدی = ransom/sacrifice). پیش ایرانیان is a locative/prepositional phrase “before/in the presence of the Iranians.” Word order is emphatic but grammatical: verb (کنم) with the noun phrase (تن فدی) specifying what is given/suffered.
segments.s212-g93: ازین is the compound of از + این “from this,” giving a locative sense (“from this battlefield”). رزمگه is the noun “battlefield.” برنگردانم is a negative first‑person verb form meaning “I will not turn back / I will not return” (بر‑ن‑گردان‑م). اسپ is the direct object “(my) horse/steed” — together: “I will not turn my horse away from this battlefield.”
segments.s212-g94: مگر here functions as ‘unless’ introducing the exceptional/counterfactual condition. خاک جایم بود is a verbless conditional clause literally “the earth were my place” (خاک = earth/dust; جایم = my place; بود = ‘were/was’ used to express the hypothetical). چون زرسپ is a simile “as with Zarasp” (چون = like/as; زرسپ = the name Zarasp), so the whole clause: “unless the earth were my place as with Zarasp.”
segments.s212-g95: اگر introduces a conditional “if.” من شوم کشته is a periphrastic way to say “if I am slain/killed” (من + شوم (optative/subjunctive) + کشته (killed) describing the resultant state). زین رزمگاه should mean “in this battlefield,” but the given senses for زین (saddle/harness) do not supply the adverbial sense; the intended reading is locative (“in/at this battlefield”), so the provided lemma senses do not cover that here.
segments.s212-g96: تو is the (emphatic) second‑person subject “you” addressing someone to act. برکش is an imperative/command form meaning “lift/bring/carry” (the provided nominal senses do not match this verbal use). سوی is the preposition “toward/to,” شاه ایران is the addressee “the King of Iran,” and سپاه is the object “the army” — overall: a command “You, bring the army toward the King of Iran.”
segments.s212-g57: بیفگند is an archaic/poetic past-form of افکندن (to throw/cast) with the prefixed ب‑ typical of older Persian narrative; the verb appears at the start (verb‑initial narrative order). دستش = دست + enclitic ش (his) marks possession. بشمشیر تیز places the instrument (بـ + شمشیر = ‘with/onto the sword’) plus the adjective تیز (sharp) after it; together they mean he cast his hand onto/at his sharp sword (i.e. seized/drew it).
segments.s212-g58: یکی باد = ‘a wind’ (one wind); برخاست is simple past ‘rose/rose up’. چون introduces a simile: رستخیز (Resurrection/uprising) is the object of the comparison. The clause describes the suddenness/force of the wind by comparing it to the Day of Resurrection.
segments.s212-g59: ز روی هوا is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘from the face/surface of the sky’ (ز = from, روی = face/surface, هوا = sky/air). ابر تیره = ‘a dark cloud’. ببرد is the narrative form of بردن (to bear/take); here it functions as a past narrative verb meaning ‘was borne’ or ‘took shape/appeared’—an older epic inflection with the prefixed ب‑.
segments.s212-g60: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase ‘came down/descended’ (فرود = descent/landing, آمد = came). از کوه رهام = ‘from Roham’s mountain’ (از = from, کوه = mountain, رهام a proper name). گرد here means ‘around/about’ and indicates the cloud descended and spread about that mountain.
segments.s212-g61: یکی دست = ‘a hand’ (one/one of the hands). با زور جادو = a prepositional phrase ‘by/with the force of magic’ (با = with, زور = force/strength, جادو = magic). بدست here functions as ‘into the (one's) hand / to hand’—it anticipates/becomes part of the following predicate (i.e. the hand came into reach). The whole segment sets up that a magically produced hand reached them.
segments.s212-g62: بهامون شد = ‘came to/for us’ or ‘became to us’ (بهامون = to us, شد = became/happened) — it completes the verbal idea that the hand came into their possession/reach. و = and; بارگی برنشست = ‘a baragi sat upon (us/it)’ (برنشست is past ‘sat upon’). In context بارگی conveys the sense of a weight/burden or oppressive thing that alighted, so the clause describes an oppressive settlement following the magical hand’s arrival.
segments.s212-g63: هوا گشت = ‘the air turned/became’ (گشت = became/turned). زان سان = ‘in that manner/so’ (زان = from/that, سان = manner/so). که از پیش بود = a relative clause ‘which/who had been before’ (که = that/which, از پیش = previously/before, بود = was—past copula). Together: the weather returned to how it had been earlier.
segments.s212-g64: فروزنده خورشید = ‘the radiant/brightening sun’ (فروزنده modifying خورشید). را is the definite/direct‑object/ topical marker but here marks the sun’s face as the thing shown; رخ نمود = ‘showed its face/appeared’ (رخ = face, نمود = appeared/showed). The clause says the bright sun revealed its face — i.e. sunlight returned.
segments.s212-g121: بپیش is a locative adverb ('toward the front') placed before the phrase; اندرون + خون literally 'within/between (the lines) blood' (اندرون governs the following noun). همی before the verb is the archaic/progressive marker that makes the action ongoing, and ریختند is the past plural of ریختن ('they poured/shedded'), so the whole clause reads as an ongoing past action ('they were pouring/shedding blood in front').
segments.s212-g122: Subject یلان (heroes) begins the clause; از پس پشت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from the rear/behind' (پس + پشت reinforce the idea of the back/behind), and بگریختند is the past plural ('they fled/escaped'). The verb bears the narrative past; the prefixed بـ here appears in older usage but the sense is simple past plural.
segments.s212-g123: یکی موبدی is 'a certain mobad' (indefinite subject). طوس یل را uses را as the definite/object marker on یل ('the hero of/associated with Tūs' or 'Tūs's hero') as the object of the verb. بخواند is used here as a past narrative verb 'called/summoned/called out' (in older Persian the ب‑ prefix appears on verbs in narration); the best reading in context is 'called (out to)' or 'addressed.'
segments.s212-g124: پس پشت is an adverbial phrase 'from behind/at the rear'; تو here is the indirect object 'to you' (used without به, as is common in poetry), and گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (note: the provided sense list did not include 'said' so index is -1). جنگی نماند is quoted content meaning 'no fighting/remnant of battle remains' (جنگی = fighting/war; نماند = 'was left'/'remained not').
segments.s212-g125: نباید is an impersonal modal prohibition ('one must not'/'they must not'); it governs the verb phrase that follows, which in classical Persian often takes a subjunctive/optative form. کت is the direct object ('coat'). اندر میان ('in/into the midst') is a fixed locative phrase, and آورند is the third‑person plural form of آوردن used here after نباید—functionally a prohibitive + subjunctive construction meaning 'must not bring.'
segments.s212-g126: بجان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'at the cost of' or 'by the life of' (lit. 'to the life'); سپهبد is the affected person/title ('the spahbed', a high general). زیان آورند is a verb phrase 'they would bring/cause loss' — here آورند is used in the same subjunctive/epic-narrative sense as above and means 'would cause' or 'bring about' harm to the spahbed (i.e. it would cost him his life).
segments.s212-g127: به گیو دلیر is an indirect object phrase 'to Giv the brave' (به marks the addressee; دلیر is an epithet following the name). آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time' inserted before the reporting verb; طوس گفت is the reporting clause 'Tous said' (گفت functions as the past 'said' — again the given lexicon entries did not list 'said' so index is -1 for that token below).
segments.s212-g128: که introduces reported content ('that'). با مغز لشکر is a prepositional phrase literally 'with the army's brain' (مغز here = 'brain' or 'leadership/clever counsel' of the army). خرد نیست جفت is a compact predicate: خرد ('wisdom') precedes the copula نیست ('is not'), and جفت ('pair/match') functions as the complement — together meaning 'there is no matching wisdom' or idiomatically 'the army has no wisdom/match in wisdom.' Word order is typical of classical Persian where predicate elements can appear before the copula.
